ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103); no specific lemon black tea subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A covers tea and tea-based beverages as the closest parent category; no specific lemon black tea code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance; used as closest parent since no lemon black tea specific concept exists.

preparation_method: blended with lemon juice or lemon flavouring

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a 500 ml bottle or cup of lemon black tea, as commonly sold at Singaporean food establishments and bubble tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 14355) and lemon juice, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 09152); values blended for lemon black tea with light sugar
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lemon black tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods at the time of processing. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea (FDC 14355) and lemon juice (FDC 09152) in proportions typical of a lightly sweetened 500 ml lemon black tea (approximately 95% brewed black tea, 3% lemon juice, 2% sugar by weight). Per-100g values reflect this blend. Sugar content estimated at approximately 5g/100g for a lightly sweetened commercial preparation, consistent with typical Singaporean hawker-style lemon tea. Unsweetened variant would have ~0g sugar.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lemon black tea. GI estimated at 30 based on the GI of lightly sweetened tea beverages and the low GI of sucrose-diluted tea drinks as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The primary glycemic contribution is from added sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) diluted to approximately 5g/100g in a predominantly water-based beverage, yielding a low overall GI. Glycemic load per 500ml serving = (30 × 25g carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~15mg per 500ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Lemon black tea is very low in sodium (approximately 15 mg per 500 ml serving) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension; however, commercially prepared versions with added salt or high sugar should be consumed with awareness of total dietary sodium and caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar contributing approximately 24g of sugar per 500ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lemon black tea prepared with added sugar contributes approximately 24 g of free sugars per 500 ml serving, which can elevate postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request unsweetened or reduced-sugar versions and account for this beverage within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; black tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemon black tea contains negligible fat and saturated fat, and the black tea component provides flavonoids (theaflavins and thearubigins) that have been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol in observational studies; unsweetened or lightly sweetened versions are preferable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon Black Tea (柠檬红茶, Níng méng hóng chá) is a popular cold or hot beverage widely consumed across Singapore, China, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by brewing black tea leaves (commonly Ceylon, Assam, or a blended tea base) and combining the infusion with fresh lemon juice or lemon flavouring, typically sweetened with sugar or simple syrup. The resulting drink has a bright amber colour, a mildly astringent and tannic base from the black tea, and a refreshing citrus tartness from the lemon. It is commonly served iced in Singapore hawker centres, bubble tea shops, and Chinese restaurants, and is available in 500 ml portions as a standard serving size. Nutritionally, a lightly sweetened 500 ml serving provides approximately 100 kcal, primarily from added sugar (~25 g carbohydrate), with negligible protein and fat. The beverage is naturally low in sodium and contains small amounts of potassium and vitamin C from the lemon component. Black tea contributes polyphenolic antioxidants including theaflavins and thearubigins, which have been studied for potential cardiovascular and metabolic benefits. Clinically, the primary concern is added sugar content for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing weight; unsweetened versions are essentially calorie-free and suitable for most dietary patterns. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemon black tea?
Is lemon black tea good for diabetics?
Lemon black tea GI glycemic index
Lemon black tea vs lemon green tea nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lemon black tea (500ml, lightly sweetened) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods at the time of processing. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea and lemon juice in proportions representative of a typical lightly sweetened Singaporean lemon black tea. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for similar sweetened tea beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the ingredient proportions are well-established for this beverage category, and the primary macronutrient driver (added sugar) is well-characterised.
notes: 1. fat_g is naturally zero for this tea-based beverage; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brewed black tea data (FDC 14355). saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by the same source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 14355) as brewed black tea contains no cholesterol. 2. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as brewed tea is a liquid infusion with no intact fibre. 3. The following micronutrients are set to -1 as they are not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea with lemon at meaningful or reliable levels and are not considered primary nutritional components of this beverage: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea (plant-based beverage contains no B12); folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355 for brewed black tea. 4. glycemic_index is set to 30 as an analogous estimate for a lightly sweetened tea beverage; gi_category is low. Glycemic load per 500ml serving calculated as (30 × 25g carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5. 5. Nutrient values represent a lightly sweetened preparation (~5g sugar per 100g). Unsweetened versions would have energy_kcal ~1, carbohydrate_g ~0.3, sugar_g ~0.0. 6. The food is tagged as Chinese cuisine per the input source (field survey data, See Ya Tonight restaurant); lemon black tea is a pan-Asian beverage with strong Chinese and Hong Kong cultural associations. 7. Serving size set to 500g (500ml) consistent with the input product description. 8. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact lemon black tea ontology entry exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.

Added Malay (Teh Hitam Lemon, Teh Lemon), Tamil (எலுமிச்சை கருப்பு தேநீர், நிம்பு தேயிலை), and Hindi (नींबू काली चाय, नींबू चाय) local names and aliases to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Chinese/Singaporean cuisine.

Set glycemic_index=30 (numeric >0) and gi_category='low' per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; glycemic_load_per_serving=7.5 calculated from 500ml serving carbohydrate content.

Expanded notes to individually document every -1 sentinel value with explicit reason and source reference per g3_numeric_hygiene sentinel values documentation rule: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg each individually documented as not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 14355.
